name: Lu Dongbin
description: Lu Dongbin, also known as Lü Dongbin, is a prominent figure in Chinese Taoism, revered as one of the Eight Immortals. He is often depicted as a scholar and swordsman, embodying the ideals of both Confucianism and Taoism. His origins are shrouded in legend, with various accounts placing his birth during the Tang Dynasty. Lu Dongbin is believed to have attained immortality through the guidance of other immortals, primarily Zhongli Quan. He is a patron saint of barbers, ink makers, and those suffering from illness. His stories often involve testing the moral character of individuals and offering assistance to the deserving. Lu Dongbin's enduring popularity stems from his relatable portrayal as a flawed yet ultimately benevolent figure, embodying the potential for spiritual transformation within every person. His image is a powerful symbol of transcendence and the pursuit of enlightenment through self-cultivation and virtuous action.
subtitle: Patron of Scholars and Barbers

keyMyths.title: Lu Dongbin's Enlightenment
keyMyths.description: According to legend, Lu Dongbin was a failed scholar who encountered Zhongli Quan at an inn. Zhongli Quan used his magic to show Lu Dongbin a vivid dream of a successful and powerful life, filled with both joy and sorrow. Upon awakening, Lu Dongbin realized the futility of worldly pursuits and resolved to seek immortality. This experience cemented his commitment to Taoist practices and his eventual transformation into an immortal. This myth highlights the importance of detachment and the pursuit of spiritual enlightenment over material success.
keyMyths.source: Werner, E.T.C. *Myths and Legends of China.* George G. Harrap & Company, 1922.

keyMyths.title: The Testing of the Gold
keyMyths.description: Lu Dongbin, disguised as a beggar, would often test the compassion and virtue of people. One common story involves him leaving a lump of gold on the street. Only one individual, a poor but honest man, returned the gold to Lu Dongbin. Impressed by the man's integrity, Lu Dongbin rewarded him with the knowledge of alchemy, enabling him to create medicine to help the sick. This tale exemplifies Lu Dongbin's role as a moral judge and rewarder of virtue.
keyMyths.source: Eberhard, Wolfram. *A Dictionary of Chinese Symbols: Hidden Symbols in Chinese Life and Thought.* Routledge, 2013.

keyMyths.title: Lu Dongbin and the Dragon King's Daughter
keyMyths.description: A popular folktale recounts Lu Dongbin's encounter with the Dragon King's daughter, who was suffering from a mysterious illness. He used his alchemical skills and Taoist magic to cure her, demonstrating his power over the natural world and his ability to heal. This myth further solidifies his image as a benevolent immortal who intervenes in human affairs and offers aid to those in need, regardless of their status or origin. The story also highlights the Taoist belief in harmony between humans and nature.
keyMyths.source: Little, Stephen, and Shawn Eichman. *Taoism and the Arts of China.* Art Institute of Chicago, 2000.
